talk_title: Open-access chemical probes and Target 2035 initiative for the druggable proteome

speaker.name: Dr. Cheryl H. Arrowsmith
speaker.affiliation: Senior Scientist, Princess Margaret Cancer Centre; Professor, University of Toronto

targets.dependency_context: MLL-rearranged leukemia, p53 gain-of-function breast cancer
mutant ALL and multiple myeloma cells

MOA_summary: The SGC develops open-access chemical probes that selectively inhibit epigenetic regulators such as WDR5, PRMT5, PRMT1 and NSD2. Probe binding disrupts protein–protein interactions or catalytic activity, leading to transcriptional reprogramming, loss of oncogenic histone marks, and selective growth suppression in cancer models dependent on these epigenetic pathways. A PROTAC-derived NSD2 degrader recruits FBXO22, forming a covalent adduct that eliminates NSD2 protein and its H3K36me2 mark, restoring dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cells.
key_findings: Developed ~200 high‑quality chemical probes, including 50–70 epigenetic modulators, all freely available to the community.
Identified OICR-9429 as a potent, selective WDR5‑MLL interaction inhibitor that suppresses growth of MLL‑rearranged leukemia and p53‑gain‑of‑function breast cancer cells.
Discovered a PROTAC that recruits FBXO22 to degrade NSD2, eliminating H3K36me2 and selectively killing NSD2‑mutant ALL and multiple myeloma cells after 18–21 days.
NSD2 degradation restored dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cell lines, suggesting a combinatorial therapeutic strategy.
Machine‑learning‑guided DNA‑encoded library screens across 18 WDR proteins yielded low‑micromolar, ligand‑efficient hits for ~1/3 of the targets, expanding the druggable proteome.

combination_strategies.rationale: NSD2 degradation re‑sensitizes mutant ALL cells to standard steroid therapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: restored dexamethasone‑induced cell death in NSD2‑mutant lines
